1: Jeremiah prophesied ca. 627-586 BCE (from the 13th year of Josiah through the fall of Jerusalem and beyond), the most turbulent period in Judah's history. He witnessed Josiah's reform (621 BCE, 2 Kings 22-23) and its collapse, the defeats at Megiddo (609 BCE) and Carchemish (605 BCE), Nebuchadnezzar's three deportations (605, 597, 586 BCE), the destruction of Jerusalem and the temple (586 BCE), and the assassination of Gedaliah. His call at the outset of his ministry and his suffering throughout (the 'Confessions', Jer 11-20) make him the most personal of the prophets — his inner life is more visible in Scripture than any other OT figure. The 'new covenant' oracle (Jeremiah 31:31-34) is addressed to a people in the ruins of the Babylonian exile.
2: 'The word of the LORD came to him in the days of Josiah son of Amon, king of Judah, in the thirteenth year of his reign' — 627/626 BCE, approximately four years before Josiah's great reform (621 BCE, triggered by the discovery of the Torah scroll in the temple: 2 Kings 22). Jeremiah's call therefore precedes the deuteronomic reform; his entire early ministry overlaps with the reform period, making the later resumption of apostasy under Jehoiakim (609 BCE) all the more devastating. The dating formula grounds Jeremiah's words in verifiable historical time — a standard feature of eighth-and-seventh-century prophetic books that distinguishes them from timeless wisdom literature.
3: 'It came again in the days of Jehoiakim ... continuing through the eleventh year of Zedekiah ... until Jerusalem was carried away captive in the fifth month' — the book's temporal frame spans three reigns and culminates in the Babylonian exile of 586 BCE. The 'fifth month' (Ab = July/August) is the month the first temple was destroyed (2 Kings 25:8-9), which became the annual fast of Tisha B'Av in later Judaism. Jeremiah's career thus covers Judah's entire final generation — from reform to catastrophe — making him the witness-prophet of the covenant's ultimate failure under the old structure.
4: 'The word of the LORD came to me' — the call-vision narrative (Jeremiah 1:4-19) follows the standard OT call-narrative structure: divine address → human protest → divine reassurance → sign → commission (cf. Moses: Exod 3-4; Isaiah: Isa 6; Ezekiel: Ezek 1-3; Gideon: Judges 6:11-24). The formula 'the word of the LORD came to me' (wayĕhî dĕbar-Yhwh ʾēlay) is Jeremiah's characteristic prophetic authorization formula, appearing over 150 times in the book. It distinguishes prophetic speech from wisdom teaching or royal decree — the authority is not learned but received.
5: 'Before I formed you in the womb I knew you, and before you were born I consecrated you; I appointed you a prophet to the nations' — the pre-birth call is the most extreme form of prophetic election in the OT. The verb yādaʿ (know) here carries the relational sense of covenantal intimacy (as in Genesis 18:19: 'I have known him [Abraham], so that he may direct his children'). Qiddaštî (I consecrated/set apart) is the dedication language used for the Nazirite vow and temple vessels — Jeremiah is consecrated as a dedicated instrument before birth. 'Prophet to the nations' (lĕgôyim, plural) is surprising: Jeremiah's mandate is not merely to Judah but to the international political order.
6: 'Ah, Lord GOD! I do not know how to speak, for I am only a child' — the call-resistance follows a recognized genre. Moses: 'I am not eloquent' (Exodus 4:10); Isaiah: 'I am a man of unclean lips' (Isaiah 6:5); Gideon: 'my clan is the weakest in Manasseh, and I am the least in my family' (Judges 6:15). The objection establishes that the prophet is not a self-appointed speaker — he is sent despite personal inadequacy, which grounds the authority in divine initiation rather than human qualification. 'I am only a child' (naʿar ʾānōkî) probably means 'young man' (15-20 years old if the 627 BCE date is accurate) rather than an infant, though the exact age is disputed.
7: 'Do not say, “I am a child,” for you shall go to everyone I send you to' — the divine counter to the protest uses the same two elements as Exodus 3-4: the commission overrides the objection, and the 'I am with you' promise (Jeremiah 1:8) replaces the inadequacy. The chiastic structure is precise: 'Do not say X (I can't speak) / because Y (you shall speak / you shall go)' — the divine mandate redefines what is possible. The repetition of 'everything I command you' establishes the prophetic mandate as a delivery function: Jeremiah does not select the message but transmits it faithfully.
8: 'Do not be afraid of them, for I am with you to rescue you, declares the LORD' — the reassurance formula 'I am with you' (ʾittĕkā ʾānî) is the same presence-promise given to the patriarchs (Genesis 26:24; Jeremiah 28:15), Moses (Exodus 3:12), and Joshua (Joshua 1:5,9). Its appearance here situates Jeremiah in the covenant-leader succession. 'To rescue you' (lĕhaṣṣîlĕkā, to deliver/snatch away) anticipates the book's pattern: Jeremiah will face constant opposition, imprisonment, and assassination attempts (Jeremiah 11, 20, 26, 36-37), and YHWH's rescue will be his repeated experience.
9: 'Then the LORD reached out his hand and touched my mouth' — the mouth-touch is the physical counterpart to the verbal commission: the divine hand places the word in the prophet's mouth. The closest parallel is Isaiah 6:6-7, where a seraph touches Isaiah's lips with a live coal and declares his guilt removed. Here the gesture is direct: no intermediary, no purification — YHWH's hand directly consecrates Jeremiah's speech organ. The superscription's 'the words of Jeremiah' (Jeremiah 1:1) and the formula 'I have placed my words in your mouth' create a dual authorship claim: the book is Jeremiah's in form but YHWH's in content.
10: 'I have appointed you this day over nations and kingdoms, to uproot and to tear down, to destroy and to demolish, to build and to plant' — the six-verb mandate (four destructive, two constructive) defines the character of Jeremiah's prophetic ministry. The ratio (Jeremiah 4:2) reflects the book's dominant tone: the judgment oracles far outnumber the restoration promises, though the restoration material (Jeremiah 30-33: the 'Book of Consolation') is among the most extended in the prophets. 'Over nations and kingdoms' makes Jeremiah a geo-political prophet whose words function as divine decrees over the international order — the Babylonian invasions are the fulfillment of this mandate.
11: 'What do you see, Jeremiah? I see a branch of an almond tree' — the vision reports follow the standard prophetic vision-dialogue form found in Amos 7-8 (the locust, fire, plumb line, summer fruit) and Zechariah 1-6 (the horses, horns, man with measuring line). The almond tree (šāqēd) was the first tree to blossom in spring (January/February), making it a symbol of watchful anticipation. In Hebrew, šāqēd (almond) creates a deliberate sound-play with šōqēd (watching/wakeful: Jeremiah 1:12), exploiting the root SQD.
12: 'You have seen rightly, for I am watching over my word to carry it out' — the word-play: šāqēd (almond) : šōqēd (I am watching). The pun is the point of the vision: the almond-branch is not allegorical content but a mnemonic trigger for the divine watchfulness-claim. YHWH's wakeful vigilance over his announced word guarantees its fulfillment. This is a key claim in the face of prophetic skepticism: the word of judgment does not return void (Isaiah 55:11) — YHWH stays wakeful over it until it is enacted.
13: 'I see a boiling pot, with its mouth turned from the north' — the boiling pot (sîr nāpûaḥ) facing south (i.e., its contents will spill southward out of the north) represents the impending military invasion from Babylon. The north-as-source-of-disaster was an established geographic-political reference: Assyria and Babylonia both invaded from the north (the direct east-west route across the Fertile Crescent). In the ancient imagination, the north was the direction of cosmic threat (Isaiah 14:13: the king of Babylon seeking 'the far north' as the mountain of the gods) as well as political danger.
14: 'Disaster will break out from the north against all who live in this land' — the 'foe from the north' (mîṣṣāpôn) is one of Jeremiah's most developed themes (Jeremiah 4:6; Jeremiah 6:1,22; Jeremiah 10:22; Jeremiah 13:20; Jeremiah 25:9). In the early chapters (1-6), the northern foe is unnamed — a deliberate rhetorical choice that maintains threat-ambiguity and maximum pressure. Scholars debate whether the early 'foe from the north' oracles referred to the Scythian raids of the late seventh century (Herodotus 1.104-106) or always to Babylon. The later identification of Babylon as YHWH's instrument (Jeremiah 25:9: 'my servant Nebuchadnezzar') retroactively clarifies the earlier oracles.
15: 'For see, I am summoning all the peoples of the kingdoms of the north ... They will come and will each set up his throne at the entrance to the gates of Jerusalem' — the military picture is precise: foreign kings setting their thrones at the gate meant establishing judicial authority over the conquered city (cf. Jeremiah 39:3: the Babylonian officials sitting in the Middle Gate after the fall of Jerusalem). The gate as the seat of legal authority (Deuteronomy 21:19; Ruth 4:1-2) makes this image doubly humiliating: the foreign invaders will occupy Israel's civic-judicial space.
16: 'I will pronounce my judgments against them for all their wickedness — they have forsaken me, burned incense to other gods' — the covenant lawsuit formula: YHWH as judge pronouncing sentence against the covenant-violating people. The three charges (forsaking YHWH / burning incense to other gods / bowing to idols) correspond to the three prohibitions of the Decalogue's first commandment complex (Exodus 20:3-5). Burning incense (qiṭṭēr) to other gods was the most common form of popular idolatry in the ANE — incense offerings were made to Baal, Asherah, and the host of heaven (2 Kings 23:5; Jeremiah 44:17-19).
17: 'As for you, get yourself ready. Arise and speak to them everything I command you. Do not be terrified by them, or I will terrify you before them' — the double-terror warning is striking: if Jeremiah allows human fear to silence the divine word, YHWH will bring divine terror upon him instead. This inverts the usual prophetic reassurance: the expected formula is 'do not fear, for I am with you'; here the prophet is warned that cowardice has its own divine consequence. The word 'terrified' uses ʾal-tēḥat (from ḥātat, to be shattered/broken), the same root used for the militarily defeated and for the psychologically crushed.
18: 'See, I have made you today a fortified city, an iron pillar, and bronze walls' — the metaphors of architectural impregnability describe the prophet's divinely sustained endurance against social opposition. A 'fortified city' (ʿîr miḇṣār) was the highest military designation in the ANE — a city able to withstand siege. 'Iron pillar' and 'bronze walls' add the additional images of vertical structural integrity and defensive perimeter. The prophet does not become physically invulnerable (he will be beaten, imprisoned, and thrown in a cistern) but spiritually sustained against the crushing social pressure his message will generate.
19: 'They will fight against you, but they will not overcome you, for I am with you to rescue you, declares the LORD' — the divine-presence promise closes the call narrative as it opened it (Jeremiah 1:8): 'I am with you to rescue you.' The inclusio creates the call's theological frame: the entire commission (Jeremiah 1:9-18) is bracketed by the presence-promise. The Jeremiah narrative (Jeremiah 26-45) will repeatedly show this promise tested: Jeremiah faces execution multiple times (Jeremiah 26:10-11; Jeremiah 38:4-6), but the word 'they will not overcome you' stands throughout — not through ease but through sustained divine protection.
